coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and bromine co-doped carbon dots with red fluorescence for sensing of Ag+ and visual monitoring of glutathione in cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiantao
Shen, Mengxin
Shen, Jiwei
Wang, Chaozhan
Wei, Yinmao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with red fluorescence emission have excellent advantages in cell imaging. Herein, novel nitrogen and bromine doped CDs (N,Br-CDs) were prepared with 4-bromo-1,2-phenylenediamine as precursor. The N, Br-CDs present the optimal emission wavelength at 582 nm (λex = 510 nm) at pH 7.0 and 648 nm (λex = 580 nm) at pH 3.0 ∼ 5.0, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of N,Br-CDs at 648 nm versus Ag+ concentration shows a good relationship from 0 to 60 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ is efficiently restored via the combination of glutathione (GSH) and Ag+ and linearly changes with GSH concentration from 0 ∼ 6.0 μM with LOD of 49 nM. This method has been successfully employed to monitor intracellular Ag+ and GSH with fluorescence imaging. The results suggest that the N,Br-CDs has application potential in the sensing of Ag+ and visual monitoring of GSH in cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Long emission wavelength
Sensing of Ag+ and GSH
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Responsiveness of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system toward biothiols
Response mechanism of N,Br-CDs to Ag+ and GSH
Application of N,Br-CDs in intracellular imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974106 , 22274129 and 22074117 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM image (inset). (B) Size distribution of N,Br-CDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of 4-bromo-1,2-phenylenediamine (a) and N,Br-CDs (b). (D) Full XPS survey scan of N,Br-CDs.
High-resolution of N,Br-CDs. (A) C 1s; (B) N 1s; (C) O 1s; (D) Br 3d.
Fluorescence emission spectra of N,Br-CDs under the different excitation wavelengths and different pH. (A) pH 3.0; (B) pH 4.0; (C) pH 5.0; (D) pH 7.0.

(A) Fluorescence response of N,Br-CDs to Ag+ (50 μM) and other metal ions (1 mM) in phosphate buffer (pH 5.0). (B) Fluorescence response of N,Br-CDs in the mixture of Ag+ (50 μM) and interfering ions (1 mM). (C) Fluorescence spectrum of N,Br-CDs upon adding various Ag+ concentration. (D) Plot of (F0-F)/F0 against Ag+ concentrations. Excitation/emission wavelengths at 580/648 nm.

(A) Fluorescence response of the N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system containing amino acids and anions. (B) Emission spectra of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ titrated with GSH (100 μM). (C) Relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration of GSH. (D) Relationship between log [FL] and log [GSH]. Substances: 0, Ag+; 1, S2- (50 μM); 2, GSH (5 μM); 3, Hcy (25 μM); 4, l-cys (25 μM); 5, glycine; 6, glutamic acid; 7, methionine; 8, arginine; 9, leucine; 10, proline; 11, tryptophan; 12, Serine; 13, threonine; 14, asparagine; 15, valine; 16, histidine; 17, ascorbic acid; 18, phosphate; 19, sulfate; 20, nitrate; 21, nitrite. The concentrations of other analytes were all 500 μM.
Fluorescence images of the N,Br-CDs (20 μg/mL), 1 mM NEM and different concentration of Ag+ incubated with HeLa cells. (A) 0 μM, (B) 25 μM, (C) 50 μM, and (D) 100 μM Ag+.
Fluorescence images of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ (100 μM), 1 mM NEM and different concentration of GSH incubated with HeLa cells. (A) 0 μM, (B) 1 μM, (C) 3 μM, and (D) 5 μM GSH.
The fluorescence probe based the N,Br-CDs for Ag+ and GSH.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and bromine co-doped carbon dots with red fluorescence for sensing of Ag+ and visual monitoring of glutathione in cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lanzhou Petrochemical Research Center, Petrochemical Research Institute, PetroChina, Lanzhou 730060, Gansu, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lanzhou Petrochemical Research Center, Petrochemical Research Institute, PetroChina, Lanzhou 730060, Gansu, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with red fluorescence emission have excellent advantages in cell imaging. Herein, novel nitrogen and bromine doped CDs (N,Br-CDs) were prepared with 4-bromo-1,2-phenylenediamine as precursor. The N, Br-CDs present the optimal emission wavelength at 582 nm (λex = 510 nm) at pH 7.0 and 648 nm (λex = 580 nm) at pH 3.0 ∼ 5.0, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of N,Br-CDs at 648 nm versus Ag+ concentration shows a good relationship from 0 to 60 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescence of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ is efficiently restored via the combination of glutathione (GSH) and Ag+ and linearly changes with GSH concentration from 0 ∼ 6.0 μM with LOD of 49 nM. This method has been successfully employed to monitor intracellular Ag+ and GSH with fluorescence imaging. The results suggest that the N,Br-CDs has application potential in the sensing of Ag+ and visual monitoring of GSH in cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been aroused considerable concerns because of tunable fluorescence characteristics, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, good water dispersbility, low toxicity and environment friendly [1]. Compared with the traditional fluorescent materials, CDs are potential in the fields of fluorescence biosensing and bioimaging. Therefore, much efforts have been put into developing the preparation methods of CDs [2,3]. Among these methods, hydrothermal method is simple, green and effective [4]. Compared with the availability of CDs, the improvement of fluorescence characteristics, selectivity and sensitivity is a hard work. At present, the emission wavelengths of most reported CDs are located in blue to yellow light regions. However, the short emission wavelengths give rise to strong tissue absorption, shallow tissue penetration, high interference from auto-fluorescence and severe photodamage to tissues and cells in bioimaging and biosensing [5]. To avoid the problems, it is of significance to prepare intensive red/near-infrared emitting CDs under long-wavelength excitation.

In general, the fluorescence origin of CDs is due to radiative transition between the fluorescence centers, which include heteroatom containing double bonds and single bonds [6]. The heteroatoms can regulate the fluorescence characteristics of CDs through particular quantum confinement, edge effects and surface passivation [7]. Besides, the fluorescence properties of CDs can be also affected by conjugative effect between fluorophore and unsaturated bonds [8]. For the reasons, the compounds containing unsaturated bonds and heteroatoms are regarded as important carbon sources to synthesize long wavelength emission CDs. For instance, the N doped CDs prepared from phenylnediamine emitted wavelength of 616 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 15.8 % [9]; Sulfur doped CDs made with polythiophene as precursor had an emission wavelength of 640 nm with the QY of only 2.3 % [10]; The neutral red and citric acid as carbon sources CDs yielded an emission wavelength of 632 nm and QY of 12.1 % [11]. Although there were a few of red-emissive CDs, most CDs had lower QYs and shorter excitation wavelengths in the practical biological application [12,13].

Silver and glutathione (GSH) are two substances that need to be detected frequently in biological samples. Silver is used in the field of photography, gems, electronic devices and biomedicine. However, the ingested Ag+ can cause a series of diseases and also can replace indispensable metal ions (Ca2+ and Zn2+) in bones, posing a major threat to human health [14,15]. As for GSH, it can participate in various oxidative reactions and intracellular signal transduction, protect the enzymes activity and prevent apoptosis [16]. Of the detection methods of two analytes, the fluorescent analysis is simple, highly sensitive and selective [17]. The reported fluorescent substances include semiconductor quantum dots [18,19], metal nanoclusters [20,21] and organic small molecules [22,23]. Compared with these probes, CDs are low-cost and simple to be prepared. Accordingly, the CDs as probes for Ag+ and GSH have been developed, i.g. N, S co-doped CDs [24], UCDs [25] and N-CDs [26]. However, theses CDs possess short-wavelength emission, severe synthetic conditions and low fluorescence quantum yield. In addition, the CDs were necessarily used in neutral or alkaline buffers, in which GSH is easily oxidized, lowering the measurement accuracy.

Herein, a nitrogen and bromine doped CDs (N,Br-CDs) were obtained by solvothermal method with 4-bromine-1,2-phenylenediamine as carbon source. Helpfully, N,Br-CDs emit red fluorescence of 648 nm (excited at 580 nm) in acidic solution and are directly acted as a fluorescence sensing platform for Ag+. Also, the quenched intensity of N,Br-CDs by Ag+ can be effectively restored through the competitive reaction of GSH and Ag+. Based on the property, the fluorescence detection of intracellular GSH was also established under pH 5.0 (Scheme 1 ). Notably, the acidic medium avoids the oxidation of GSH and improves the detection accuracy.

200 mg of 4-bromine-1,2-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol and the solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave. The autoclave was heated in oven at 180° C for 12 h. The reactant was automatically cooled to room temperature and was purified through silica gel column chromatography (ethyl acetate: petroleum ether eluent, 3: l). The black solid was obtained after concentration.

In the detection of Ag+, 200 μL of N,Br-CDs (1.0 mg/mL in ethanol) were added into 1500 µL of phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 5.0). Then, Ag+ solutions with different concentrations were introduced. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured.

As for the detection of GSH, a series of GSH with different concentrations were mixed with the N,Br-CDs/Ag+ (100 μM) solution and fluorescence spectra were recorded. To evaluate the selectivity of N,Br-CDs towards Ag+ and GSH, different metal ions and amino acids were added into N,Br-CDs and the solution was measured by fluorescence spectrum. All tests were repeated three times.

HeLa cells were incubated in fresh cellular medium at 37℃under 5 % CO2 for 48 h. The cellular medium was removed and the cells were repeatedly washed by PBS (pH 5.0). The cells were inoculated in the culture dish containing 1.0 mM N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) for 30 min to exhaust the intracellular biothiols and further incubated with 1.0 mL of N,Br-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 3 h. After rinsed 3 times with 1.0 mL of buffer to remove excess N,Br-CDs, the cells were cultured in the Ag+ solution (25, 50, 100 μM) for 30 min. The cells were visualized using confocal microscope.

To image intracellular GSH, the cells were successively incubated with N,Br-CDs, NEM and Ag+(100 μM) as the above method. Then the cells were incubated with GSH (0, 1, 3 and 5 μM) for 30 min. After rinsed with buffer solution, the cells were visualized using confocal microscope.

Fluorescence emission wavelengths of most reported CDs for Ag+ ranged in short-wavelength emission region, which restricts their applications in bioanalysis. To solve the problem, 4-bromo-1,2-phenylenediamine was used as precursor to synthesize the red fluorescence N,Br-CDs, for which emission wavelengths can be regulated by the oxidation state of the surface and the level of heteroatomic doping. TEM image shows that the N,Br-CDs are mono-dispersed nanoparticles with an average particle size of 2.1 nm (Fig. 1 A and B). Meanwhile, the N,Br-CDs have good dispersibility. HRTEM image shows that the lattice spacing of N,Br-CDs is approximately 0.21 nm (Fig. 1A), corresponding to the (100) facet of graphene-carbon nucleus [27].

FT-IR was used to characterize the surface groups of N,Br-CDs. In Fig. 1C, the absorption peaks at 3415 and 3375 cm−1 correspond to OH and NH, respectively [28]. The two peaks at 3026 and 2981 cm−1 belong to CH stretching vibrations. The amide I and II bond stretching vibration of CO are found at 1733, 1618 and 1533 cm−1, respectively [29]. The adsorption at 1473 cm−1 is assigned to NH bending vibration [30]. The two peaks at 1386 and 1056 cm−1 are the -CN and CO stretching vibration [31]. The adsorption at 620 cm−1 is assigned to aromatic C-Br bond [32]. Hence, the N,Br-CDs are endowed with amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

The chemical composition of N, Br-CDs surface was confirmed with XPS (Fig. 1D). There are mainly-four peaks at 68.0, 284.0, 399.0 and 530.0 eV, which belong to Br 3d, C 1s, N 1s and O1s, respectively. The contents of four elements are 2.76 %, 77.69 %, 11.18 % and 8.37 %. In Fig. 2 A, there are four peaks of C 1s at 284.6 eV (CC/CC), 285.6 eV (CN), 286.7 eV (CO), 288.0 eV (OCO) [33]. In addition, N 1 s high-resolution spectrum presents four peaks at 397.50, 398.40, 399.50 and 400.40 eV (Fig. 2B), which belong to graphite nitrogen, pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and amine nitrogen, located respectively [34,35]. Furthermore, the two peaks in O 1 s spectrum are attributed to –CON- (531.30 eV) and CO (532.29 eV) (Fig. 2C) [36]. For Br 3d high-resolution spectrum, there are three peaks (Fig. 2D), which are assigned to C-Br 3d5/2 (72.53 eV), CBr 3d3/2 (71.12 eV) and Br- (68.4 eV), respectively [32]. The results suggest that nitrogen and bromine are successfully doped into N,Br-CDs and the rich groups are amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. It's worth noting that these groups can result in the defects, which act as the excitation traps to induce the unique fluorescence properties.

The optical characteristics of N,Br-CDs were investigated with UV–vis and fluorescence emission spectra. It is found that the absorption and emission spectra of N,Br-CDs are significantly affected by pH of solution. At pH 3.0, there are four peaks at 285, 486, 578 and 627 nm in absorption spectrum (Fig. S1). The peaks at 285 and 486 nm belong to π-π* and n-π* transition of carbon nucleus while the peaks at 578 and 627 nm are attributed to pyridin-N and pyrro-N absorption peaks, respectively [37]. In neutral solution, the absorption peak at 285 nm does not shift but the other three peaks shift blue to 455, 528 and 568 nm, respectively. The shifts are attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of N,Br-CDs caused by the abundant carboxyl and nitrogen-based functional groups in N,Br-CDs. For the reason, pH of buffer has a large influence on the fluorescence spectra of N,Br-CDs (Fig. 3 ). Acidic buffer (pH 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0) can generate an optimal fluorescence emission wavelength with 648 nm. In contrast, the fluorescence wavelength is shifted to 582 nm at pH 7.0. This result suggests that the fluorescence emission of N,Br-CDs red-shifts along with the reducing pH. As stated above, there are specific electron structure on the surface of N,Br-CDs, e.g. π-conjugated carbon framework, amino (NH2), OH and COOH groups. In the acidic environment, the protonation and deprotonation of these groups can induce fluorophore evolution to yield the red fluorescence emission of N,Br-CDs [38,39]. At the same time, the energy band gap is changed, resulting in the emission wavelength shift [40].

The other essential parameters of N,Br-CDs were also measured. Specifically, QY of N,Br-CDs is up 30.0 % in ethanol. Furthermore, the optical stability of N,Br-CDs is excellent, which is proved by recording the fluorescence intensity under a Xenon lamp irradiation for 60 min (Fig. S2A). Also, the stable emission of N,Br-CDs at salt concentration range of 0 ∼ 1.0 M NaCl shows the good application under physiological environments (Fig. S2B).

It is found that Ag+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of N,Br-CDs. To explore the application of the property in the detection of Ag+, the effect of pH on the detection sensitivity was optimized. The fluorescence intensities of N,Br-CDs and N,Br-CDs/Ag+ at 648 nm show a decreasing trend as pH is varied from 3.0 to 5.0 (Fig. S3A). On the other hand, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of N,Br-CDs towards Ag+ increases as pH of solution from 3.0 to 5.0 (Fig. S3B). The fluorescence quenching efficiency by Ag+ is lower under the lower pH because the N,Br-CDs are difficult to form the complexes with Ag+ due to the protonation of surface functional groups. For the reason, pH of the solution is set at 5.0. To investigate the selectivity of N,Br-CDs towards Ag+, the influences of different monovalent, bivalent and trivalent ions on the fluorescence were investigated at pH 5.0 (Fig. 4 A). It is found that other metal ions (1 mM) have no obvious influences on the quenching efficiency of Ag+ at 648 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity has no obvious changes even if the concentration of other metal ions is up to 20 times that of Ag+ (Fig. 4B). Such a high selectivity and anti-interference ability of N,Br-CDs indicates that the proposed probe can used in the fluorescence determination of Ag+. Importantly, the fluorescence intensity of N,Br-CDs at 648 nm regularly decreases along with Ag+ concentration increase (Fig. 4C). Moreover, plot of (F0-F)/F0 against Ag+ concentration shows an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.999) from 0 ∼ 60 μM (Fig. 4D). The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.14 μM (S/N = 3). The parameters meet the requirements on the determination of Ag+.

Table S1 compares the reported fluorescence methods with the developed methods for determination of Ag+. It is found that the detection range and LOD of the developed methods are comparative or even better than the reported methods. Most noteworthy is the longer emission wavelength of N,Br-CDs, which can improve application efficiency in environmental detection.

To verify the practicability of the method, Ag+ in environmental water was detected by the standard addition method (Table S2). The recoveries of Ag+ are in the range of 88.26 ∼ 119.04 % and the RSD is lower than 2.07 %, suggesting that the possibility of N,Br-CDs as a sensitive probe of Ag+ in environmental analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responsiveness of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system toward biothiols
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering that Ag+ has a strong affinity to the biothiols containing GSH, l-cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy), the fluorescence of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system can be restored by the biothiols. Therefore, the fluorescence system can act as fluorescence probe of the thiols. To verify this expectation, the selectivity of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system was assessed. Upon adding different kinds of substances (e.g. amino acids and anions) into the N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system, only three thiols are found to efficiently restore the fluorescence of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system, whereas other substances have no interference to the fluorescence of the system (Fig. 5
A). In addition, the fluorescence restoration ability of GSH is much larger than those for Hcy and Cys. It is worth noting that the GSH-concentration in mammalian cell outclasses Cys and Hcy. For these reasons, N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system is able to selectively detect GSH excluding Cys and Hcy. The special structure of GSH molecule, which is a multidentate anchor and polybasicacid (Fig. S4), is the possible reason for the unique selectivity of the N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system. In addition, as other substances coexist with GSH, GSH can still efficiently enhance the fluorescence intensity of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ and the enhancement amplitude is not affected by the coexisting interferes (Fig. S5), further indicating that N,Br-CDs/Ag+ possesses an excellent selectivity towards GSH in the complex solution. Furthermore, the intensity of N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system is gradually enhanced with increasing GSH concentration (Fig. 5B and C) and plot of the intensity against GSH concentration from 0.2 ∼ 6 μM yields a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.993) (Fig. 5D). The LOD of GSH is determined to be 43 nM (S/N = 3). In a word, a viable fluorescence system for GSH measurement is successfully constructed based the N,Br-CDs/Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of N,Br-CDs to Ag+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the response mechanism of N,Br-CDs to Ag+, the zeta potential, UV–vis spectroscopy and lifetime measurement were conducted. After adding Ag+ into the N,Br-CDs solution, the zeta potential of the N,Br-CDs goes down from 16.9 to 12.1 mV (Fig. S6A), suggesting the complexation and electrostatic interaction between Ag+ and the surface functional groups of N,Br-CDs [41]. Upon continuing to add GSH in the solution, the zeta potential of the N,Br-CDs falls to −14.6 mV. As for UV–vis spectroscopy, the absorption bands at 580 nm and 628 nm are blue shifted after adding Ag+, further suggesting that there is the binding interaction between N,Br-CDs and Ag+ (Fig. S6B). Also, the binding constant (K) between N, Br-CDs and Ag+ is determined according to the Lineweaver-Burk equation [42]:
1
F 0
- F
=
1
F 0
+
1
K
F 0
Q

As depicted from Fig. S7A, K is calculated to be 1.23 × 104 M−1, suggesting the strong binding interaction between N,Br-CDs and Ag+ [43]. The strong affinity for Ag+ is due to the presence of the surface functional groups of N,Br-CDs, i.e. NH2, COO-, OH. The affinity can very easily cause the excited electrons transfer from N,Br-CDs to the unoccupied orbital of Ag+ via nonradioactive electron-transfer process. Furthermore, the lifetime of N,Br-CDs is measured to be 1.99 ns before addition of Ag+, but it decreases to 1.53 ns upon introducing Ag+ (Fig. S7B), suggesting a dynamic quenching occurred [44,45]. As GSH was added to the N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system, the lifetime of N, Br-CDs recovers to 1.98 ns because of the drop of Ag+ from the N,Br-CDs-Ag+ to regain the N,Br-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N,Br-CDs in intracellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: GSH exists in almost cells, can help maintain the normal function of the immune system and has an integrated detoxification effect through the combination with certain drugs and toxins. Besides, the excretion of free radical can be promoted through the direct reduction of free radical into acidic substances by sulfydryl. Therefore, the monitoring of GSH concentration level in cellular environmental is of significance. Before the application of N,Br-CDs in the biological imaging is investigated, its cytotoxicity was firstly assessed with HeLa cells (Fig. S8). Nearly 82 % cells survive after culturing HeLa cells under various concentration of N,Br-CDs (0 ∼ 200 μg/mL), demonstrating that the N,Br-CDs possess low toxicity.

The cell imaging experiments using N,Br-CDs were carried out in vitro. Firstly, HeLa cells were pretreated with N,Br-CDs and various concentrations Ag+ in sequence. The confocal microscopy images were collected after HeLa cells were rinsed with the PBS. It is found that the N,Br-CDs exhibit intense intracellular red fluorescence (Fig. 6A). The intracellular fluorescence is gradually weakened with increasing Ag+ concentration (Fig. B ∼ D), indicating that the probe based the N,Br-CDs has a high sensitivity for the intracellular Ag+.

Most importantly, GSH can effectively restore the quenched fluorescence caused by Ag+. As shown in Fig. 7, the intracellular fluorescence of the system is gradually restored to the original fluorescence upon continuously adding GSH into the HeLa cells pretreated by N,Br-CDs and Ag+. Even the GSH as low as 1.0 μM also enhances the intracellular fluorescence, further indicating the satisfactory sensitivity of the N,Br-CDs/Ag+ system in the determination of intracellular GSH. Meanwhile the N,Br-CDs possess the hypotoxicity and good biocompatibility, so it can monitor the intracellular GSH through fluorescence imaging.

A novel red emission N,Br-CDs used as a multifunctional probe were prepared by using 4-bromo-1, 2-phenylenediamine as carbon precursor. The fluorescence spectrum of the N,Br-CDs depends on pH of solution due to the surface protonated functional groups. The N,Br-CDs has good selectivity to Ag+ and anti-interference. In particular, it can be used in the assay of Ag+ and GSH in acidic solution, avoiding the oxidation of GSH in alkaline solution. The developed fluorescence sensing platform is successfully applied in the detect of Ag+ in natural water and the fluorescence imaging of intracellular Ag+ and GSH. Compared with some reported methods, the N,Br-CDs avoid the auto-fluorescence interference of biological samples and the oxidation of GSH and can achieve more accurate measurement in bioanalysis.

Jiantao Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Mengxin Shen: Software. Jiwei Shen: Writing – review & editing. Chaozhan Wang: Resources. Yinmao Wei: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974106, 22274129 and 22074117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122642.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Carbon
